speaker: Host
text_en: Anders Hegelberg is one of the biggest loving legends in the tech industry. He created Turbo Pascal, Delphi, C Sharp, and TypeScript. The impact he has had on programming languages and developer tools is immense. Today with Anders we discuss how C Sharp might have not been born if it was not for the Sun versus Microsoft lawsuit over Java.
text: Anders Hegelberg 是科技行业最受人喜爱的传奇人物之一。他创造了 Turbo Pascal、Delphi、C Sharp 和 TypeScript。他对编程语言和开发者工具产生了极其深远的影响。今天我们会和 Anders 聊聊，如果不是 Sun 和 Microsoft 围绕 Java 的那场诉讼，C Sharp 也许根本不会诞生。

speaker: Host
text_en: The behind the scenes story of TypeScript and why open sourcing it was a huge deal inside of Microsoft. What he's learned from 40 years of designing languages, including why IDs and programming languages go hand in hand, and many more. If you want a behind the scenes look at how three of the most used programming languages in history got built,
text: 我们还会聊 TypeScript 幕后的故事，以及为什么把它开源在 Microsoft 内部是一件大事。他从 40 年语言设计经验中学到了什么，包括为什么 IDE 和编程语言总是密不可分，等等。如果你想了解历史上三种使用最广泛的编程语言是如何被打造出来的幕后故事，

speaker: Host
text_en: and how AI might change our usage of programming languages, this episode is for you. Before we start, I'd like to introduce our presenting sponsor for the season, Antisysys. A definite trend that I'm seeing across the industry is a lot more focused on testing, unsurprisingly, thanks to AI. We know that software is hard to test, and we also know that AI is making it worse,
text: 以及 AI 可能会如何改变我们使用编程语言的方式，那这一期就是为你准备的。开始之前，我想先介绍一下本季节目的冠名赞助商 Antithesis。我在整个行业里明显看到一个趋势：大家比以前更重视测试了，这并不意外，原因正是 AI。我们都知道软件很难测试，而我们也知道，AI 让这件事变得更糟了，

speaker: Host
text_en: thanks to producing increasingly more code and more complex code. The bottleneck is becoming reviewing the code, testing it, and trusting it. Or is it? We tend to think that code reviews are the bottleneck because you cannot scale human reviewers with token spend. But the problem actually goes beyond code review. Really, it's about verification. We know that AI cannot verify itself.
text: 因为它产出的代码越来越多，也越来越复杂。瓶颈正在变成代码审查、测试，以及你是否敢信任这些代码。或者说，真的是这样吗？我们往往觉得代码审查才是瓶颈，因为你不可能靠增加 token 开销来无限扩展人工审查者。但问题其实不只是代码审查。归根结底，这是验证的问题。我们知道，AI 无法验证它自己。

speaker: Host
text_en: To verify the correctness of AI-generated software, we would need to cache issues that traditional tests miss, including issues that we did not even think of in a code base that is changing at superhuman speed. Oh, and we need to do all of this before deploying to production. The only way to verify that software works is to run it with realistic faults. And this is exactly what Antithesis does.
text: 要验证 AI 生成的软件是否正确，我们需要捕捉到传统测试会漏掉的问题，包括那些我们甚至都没想到过的问题，而且还是在一个以超人般速度变化的代码库里。哦对了，我们还得在部署到生产环境之前把这些都做完。验证软件是否真的可用，唯一的办法就是在逼真的故障条件下运行它。而这正是 Antithesis 所做的事。

speaker: Host
text_en: You can bring the system you work on under test and verify that it works as it should. Teams at etcd and jainstreet are doing justice and I'm also starting to use Antithesis to test real-world systems. Over the season I'll be sharing a lot more on how it works and how it can help verify that a system is bug-free. In the meantime, check out antithesis.com slash pragmatic to learn more Anders,
text: 你可以把自己负责的系统纳入测试，验证它是否按预期运行。etcd 和 Jane Street 的团队就在这么做，我自己也开始用 Antithesis 来测试真实世界的系统了。在这一季节目里，我会分享更多关于它是如何工作的，以及它如何帮助验证一个系统是否没有 bug。与此同时，欢迎访问 antithesis.com slash pragmatic 了解更多。Anders，

speaker: Host
text_en: welcome to the podcast Thank you It's brilliant to meet you. You've created so many widely used programming languages, including the one that I learned first to program with Turbo Pascal. How did you get into programming?
text: 欢迎来到播客。谢谢。很高兴见到你。你创造了这么多被广泛使用的编程语言，其中还包括我最早学编程时用的 Turbo Pascal。你是怎么进入编程这个领域的？

speaker: Guest
text_en: I was lucky enough to attend a high school that, this is back in Copenhagen, that offered students access to a computer. It was one of the first high schools in Denmark to do so. And we're talking, you know, mid to late seventies now. And I sort of got bitten by it then, you know, just this idea that you could program this machine and make it do things, you know,
text: 我很幸运，上了一所高中，那是在 Copenhagen，当时学校给学生提供了接触计算机的机会。那是 Denmark 最早这么做的几所高中之一了。我们说的是七十年代中后期。然后我差不多就是从那时候开始着迷的，你知道，就是那种感觉：你可以给这台机器编程，让它去做各种事情。

speaker: Guest
text_en: the wonder of figuring out how it was put together. Of course, it was like completely ancient by modern standards. It was like this HP 2100 with 32K of ferrite core memory. You could literally open it up and see the ferrite cores. I mean, it was amazing. You know, paper tape reader and, you know, and then we got up. 1MB 14-inch hard drive, and that was just state-of-the-art.
text: 那种弄明白它是怎么组装起来的奇妙感觉。当然，按现在的标准看，它已经是非常古老的东西了。那是一台 HP 2100，只有 32K 的磁芯内存。你真的可以把机器打开，直接看到那些磁芯。我是说，太神奇了。还有纸带读取器之类的，后来我们又升级到了 1MB 的 14 英寸硬盘，那在当时已经是最先进的了。

speaker: Guest
text_en: The bootloader was on paper tape because there was no ROM in the machine, so it started up and knew nothing, and so you had to type in the instruction sequence to load the bootloader that would then load the OS off of the hard drive.
text: 引导加载程序是在纸带上的，因为那台机器里没有 ROM，所以它启动时什么都不知道。你得先手动输入一串指令，把 bootloader 载入进来，然后它再从硬盘里加载操作系统。

speaker: Host
text_en: And as a kid, what did you start to program on it? What captured your imagination?
text: 那你小时候最开始在上面编什么程序？是什么激发了你的兴趣？

speaker: Guest
text_en: Well, this was a Hewlett-Packard. So it had Fortran, which I found to be very quirky. It had a very slow, basic interpreter. But then it had Algol, Hewlett-Packard's version of Algol, which was an interesting compiler implementation because it didn't support recursion. which is kind of bizarre you know the call instruction of that machine would store
text: 嗯，那是一台 Hewlett-Packard 的机器。所以它有 Fortran，我觉得那玩意儿特别别扭。还有一个非常慢的 BASIC 解释器。不过它也有 Algol，Hewlett-Packard 自己的 Algol 版本。那个编译器实现挺有意思的，因为它不支持递归。这其实挺离谱的，你知道，那台机器的调用指令会把

speaker: Guest
text_en: the return address in the first word of the subroutine and then just execute and then to return you would jump to that indirect through that word so if you called yourself you'd just be gone forever you know so and of course there were no debuggers or anything to help you figure this out so you just had to be real careful about which algorithms you use but
text: 返回地址存到子程序的第一个字里，然后就直接执行；等到返回的时候，就通过那个字做一次间接跳转回去。所以如果你调用自己，那基本就永远回不来了。你懂吧。而且当时当然也没有什么调试器之类的东西帮你搞明白这些，所以你只能特别小心自己选用哪些算法。不过

speaker: Guest
text_en: But it was compiled to machine code and they ran, you know, and it was like you could build games, which is what we mostly did, like lunar landers and what have you kind of thing, And so, yeah, it was fun. Back then, things were so simple, right? You could see all the way to the bottom. I mean, there was just no layering and nothing. It was right on top of the hardware.
text: 不过它是编译成机器码来跑的，确实能运行。你可以拿它做游戏，这也是我们大多数时候在干的事，比如登月舱之类的游戏，诸如此类。所以，对，挺好玩的。那时候东西都特别简单，对吧？你能一路看到最底层。我的意思是，根本没有什么分层，什么都没有，就是直接贴着硬件在跑。

speaker: Host
text_en: I guess you needed to see all the way to the bottom as well, pretty much, right? Back then.
text: 我猜那时候你基本上也必须一路看到最底层，对吧？当年就是这样。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Well, I mean, it was just so simple that you could, right? And that was the beauty of those earlier. That was true with the 8-bit micros and even, you know, the early PCs and whatever, right? And then we've just added more and more layers over time. We have a lot of layers right now, that's for sure. We do.
text: 嗯，我的意思是，那时候系统就是简单到你做得到，对吧？这也是早期那些机器的魅力所在。8 位微型机是这样，甚至早期的 PC 之类的也差不多，对吧？后来我们只是随着时间不断往上加越来越多的层。现在我们的层可真不少，这点毫无疑问。确实如此。

speaker: Host
text_en: And how did you go from building games to actually building your first ever compiler? And what was that compiler? I started in 79 at the Danish Technical University.
text: 那你是怎么从做游戏，走到真正做出自己人生中第一个编译器的？那个编译器又是什么？我是在 79 年从 Danish Technical University 开始的。

speaker: Guest
text_en: At that time also, you know, this was right when 8-bit micros were starting to become available.
text: 当时也是，正好赶上 8 位微型计算机开始出现、逐渐能买到了。

speaker: Host
text_en: 8-bit microprocessors, right?
text: 8 位微处理器，对吧？

speaker: Guest
text_en: And it was usually these kits that you bought that you had to solder together yourself. And then of course they didn't work and then you have to figure out why they didn't work. So you learned a lot about the hardware too. And I bought a British Z80 based kit computer called a NASCOM and started learning assembly programming on that one.
text: 而且通常都是那种套件，你买回来得自己焊起来。然后当然了，装完往往还不能用，你还得自己找出到底哪里出了问题。所以你也会顺带学到很多硬件方面的东西。我当时买了一台英国的、基于 Z80 的套件电脑，叫 NASCOM，然后就在那台机器上开始学汇编编程。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And then I also met with some college buddies and we ended up founding a company and we had the first computer store in Copenhagen, where you could walk in and buy a computer, one of these kid computers. And later we sold Apple IIs and VIC-20s and Commodore 64s and blah, blah, blah, you know, all of those different ones, TRS-80s.
text: 后来我还和几个大学朋友一起，最后创办了一家公司。我们开了 Copenhagen 第一家电脑店，顾客可以直接走进来买电脑，就是那种套件电脑。再后来我们还卖 Apple II、VIC-20、Commodore 64 之类的，反正各种机器都有，还有 TRS-80。

speaker: Guest
text_en: So I did a lot of programming on those and found that programming was really the thing that I enjoyed. And of course they all came with Microsoft's ROM BASIC. and which was slow, but it allowed you to write programs. Um, but I always like missed having a real programming language. something like Algol or that I had been taught, right? And then my buddy, the guy that I founded the company,
text: 所以我在那些机器上做了很多编程，也发现自己真正喜欢的其实就是编程。当然，它们都自带 Microsoft 的 ROM BASIC。那个东西比较慢，但至少能让你写程序。嗯，不过我一直还是很怀念那种真正的编程语言，像我学过的 Algol 那样的，对吧？然后我那个朋友，也就是和我一起创办公司的人，

speaker: Guest
text_en: he was like, there's this new thing called Pascal. You ought to check it out. And it's even supposed to be simpler than Algol, which was actually true of every language we had created. They got increasingly simpler as time went on. And Pascal was not that hard to implement. And so I got interested in trying to do that and then wrote a little compiler that fit into a 12K ROM
text: 他就跟我说，有个新东西叫 Pascal，你应该去看看。而且据说它甚至比 Algol 还简单，这一点其实后来我们做出来的每种语言都差不多，随着时间推移都变得越来越简单。Pascal 实现起来也没那么难。于是我就开始想试着做一个，然后写了一个小编译器，能塞进 12K 的 ROM 里，

speaker: Guest
text_en: that would compile a subset of Pascal. And you could then yank out the Microsoft ROM basic and stick in our ROM instead. And then when you booted your machine, you were in this little environment where you could type in Pascal programs and run them. And that was sort of the early precursor of Turbo Pascal, if you will.
text: 它可以编译 Pascal 的一个子集。这样你就可以把 Microsoft 的 ROM BASIC 拔掉，换上我们的 ROM。然后机器一启动，你就会进入一个小环境，可以在里面输入 Pascal 程序并运行它。某种意义上说，那就是后来 Turbo Pascal 的一个早期雏形。

speaker: Host
text_en: Many years later, you joined... Borland. And I think it was in 1989. And there you created Turbo Pascal, the programming language, but also the IDE, right?
text: 很多年后，你加入了……Borland。我记得那是在 1989 年。你在那里做出了 Turbo Pascal，这门编程语言，但也做了 IDE，对吧？

speaker: Guest
text_en: Well, there's a little more to that story. In that company that we had back in Denmark, I ended up writing eventually a full implementation of Pascal for 8-bit CPM80. And then we ended up doing a joint venture with Borland, which was also a Danish company. It was originally founded in Denmark. And we We made a royalty contract where they would sell our compiler on a royalty.
text: 嗯，这里面还有一点别的故事。我们当时在丹麦那家公司里，我后来最终写出了一个面向 8-bit CPM80 的 Pascal 完整实现。之后我们和 Borland 做了一个合资项目，Borland 当时也是一家丹麦公司，最初就是在丹麦创立的。我们签了一个版税合同，由他们来卖我们的编译器，然后按版税分成。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And that's how I got involved in Borland. And we shipped that first product in 83, the first version of Turbo Pascal. And then that took off. more than any of us had expected. And eventually that ended up being the thing that I did full time.
text: 我就是这样和 Borland 产生联系的。我们在 83 年发布了第一个产品，也就是 Turbo Pascal 的第一个版本。结果它一下子火了，远超我们任何人的预期。后来这最终就成了我全职在做的事情。

speaker: Host
text_en: Why was it called Turbo Pascal? I understand you added things on top of Pascal that was there.
text: 为什么叫 Turbo Pascal？我的理解是，你是在原有 Pascal 的基础上又加了一些东西。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Well, I mean, it was called Turbo Pascal because it was fast. Back then, Turbo was like, this was like when Audi had their Quattros and their Turbos and whatever, you know, and Turbo just meant fast, And this thing was fast and super interactive, right? And so Turbo Pascal it was.
text: 嗯，叫 Turbo Pascal，主要就是因为它快。那时候 Turbo 这个词就像……当年 Audi 有 Quattro、Turbo 之类的，你懂吧，Turbo 基本就意味着快。而这个东西确实很快，而且交互性特别强。所以它就叫 Turbo Pascal。

speaker: Host
text_en: When Turbo Pascal became big, was it big just because of the compiler? Or also there was an ID, a dedicated ID for Turbo Pascal, right?
text: Turbo Pascal 做大之后，它之所以成功只是因为编译器吗？还是说也因为它有一个 IDE，一个专门给 Turbo Pascal 用的 IDE，对吧？

speaker: Guest
text_en: That was always the idea, and that goes back even to the predecessor of Turbo Pascal, this idea that it's not just a compiler. It's an experience, right? I mean, you don't just compile your programs. You also edit them. You also run them. You also debug them. You also have a runtime library. It all has to, like, fit together. You know what I mean?
text: 这其实一直都是核心思路，而且这个思路甚至可以追溯到 Turbo Pascal 的前身：它不只是一个编译器，而是一整套体验，对吧？我的意思是，你不只是编译程序，你还要编辑它们、运行它们、调试它们，还要有运行时库。所有这些东西都得彼此配合起来。你明白我的意思吧？

speaker: Guest
text_en: And so Turbo Pascal was always about building that whole cycle and try to make it as interactive as Basic was, as an interpreted language, But giving you the performance of a compiled language and the better, you know, semantics and syntax of Pascal versus Basic. And so that was sort of the idea from day one, you know, focus on the whole cycle.
text: 所以 Turbo Pascal 从一开始讲的就是把整个循环都做出来，并且尽量做到像 Basic 这种解释型语言那样有交互性，同时又给你编译型语言的性能，以及 Pascal 相比 Basic 更好的语义和语法。所以这从第一天起大概就是我们的想法：聚焦整个开发闭环。

speaker: Host
text_en: And so when you were building the compiler, you were already thinking of ways that the IDE, for example, could make sense or could have helpful features for maybe editing or debugging, for example.
text: 所以当你在做这个编译器的时候，你其实已经在想 IDE 以后可以怎么利用它了。比如说，怎么让 IDE 能理解这些信息，或者提供一些对编辑、调试之类有帮助的功能。

speaker: Guest
text_en: You know, the first versions of Turbo Pascal didn't have a debugger. You know, you would just use write-land statements and then you'd just see what happened, right? But often if you had some error and it blew up, you know, with a runtime error, We would print out the address of the runtime error, which is where was the program counter at that point.
text: 你知道，Turbo Pascal 最早的几个版本其实没有调试器。那时候你就是写点 write-ln 语句，然后看看会发生什么，对吧？但很多时候如果程序出错崩掉了，出现运行时错误，我们就会把运行时错误对应的地址打印出来，也就是当时程序计数器所在的位置。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And then we had a mode in the compiler where we would say, compile, but stop at this address. And so the compiler was real simple. It would just produce object code. And then once it hit that address, it would just say, whatever I'm syntactically looking at right now, that must have been around where the error was. So that was like how you could go to the line where the error had occurred.
text: 后来我们在编译器里加了一个模式，就是说，编译，但在这个地址停下来。所以那个编译器本身其实非常简单，它就是生成目标代码。等它走到那个地址时，它就会说，我现在正在做语法分析看到的这段东西，大概就是出错的位置附近。所以这基本上就是你当时跳到出错那一行的方法。

speaker: Guest
text_en: You know what I mean? It's not like we had like line maps or debuggers or any of that stuff. We just had the compiler and it was just easy to make it stop at a certain address, you know, in the object output and then show you where it was in the source code.
text: 你明白我的意思吧？并不是说我们那时候已经有了什么行号映射、调试器之类的东西，完全没有。我们只有编译器，而让它在目标输出里的某个地址停下来这件事很容易做到，然后它就能告诉你源码里对应的大概位置。

speaker: Host
text_en: Why do you think Turbo Pascal was so popular? I remember back again, this was my first programming experience. It was at schools. It was outside of schools for production software. And you said yourself that it spread like wildfire.
text: 你觉得 Turbo Pascal 为什么会那么受欢迎？我记得那时候，这就是我最早的编程体验。学校里在用，学校外做正式软件开发的人也在用。而且你自己也说过，它传播得像野火一样快。

speaker: Guest
text_en: It was just better than all the competition. It was faster. It was slower. smaller. Um, it was more interactive and it was also cheaper. So it was like 10 times better at a 10th of the price of the, of the competition, right? Compilers back then used to cost $500 and they were just compilers. And then you have to have an editor and blah, blah, blah.
text: 因为它就是比所有竞争对手都强。它更快、更小，也更有交互性，而且还更便宜。所以差不多就是性能好 10 倍、价格却只有竞争对手的十分之一，对吧？那时候的编译器通常要卖 500 美元，而且还只是个编译器。然后你还得另外配个编辑器之类的，诸如此类。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And it was like this whole long winded cycle of inserting different disks with compiler pass one and two and what have you. And here was this thing that just like made it all go away and you could get it for $49.95 and for $49.95 I mean heck that was worth it just to get the manuals that came with it right I mean so so there was
text: 整个流程又臭又长，你得不停地换不同的磁盘，什么编译器第一遍、第二遍之类的。而 Turbo Pascal 这个东西一下子就把这些麻烦全都干掉了，而且你只要花 49.95 美元就能买到。说真的，49.95 美元，光是它附带的那些手册就值这个价了，对吧？所以当时盗版其实很少，因为它实在太便宜了。不过说到盗版，我们当时一直有个关于俄罗斯站点授权的笑话，说我们卖了一套给俄罗斯。

speaker: Guest
text_en: very little piracy because it was so cheap although speaking of piracy we always had the joke about the the Russian site license how we sold one copy to Russia
text: 盗版很少，因为它太便宜了。不过说到盗版，我们当时一直有个关于俄罗斯站点授权的笑话：我们卖了一套给俄罗斯。

speaker: Host
text_en: and then that got copied everywhere. But after Turbo Pascal, you built Delphi, which was an even bigger setup in many ways. This was now an integrated environment for Windows development. How did you evolve ideas from Turbo Pascal and Delphi?
text: 后来这个做法到处都被照搬了。但在 Turbo Pascal 之后，你又做了 Delphi，从很多方面来说它的规模更大。这已经是一个面向 Windows 开发的集成环境了。你是怎么把 Turbo Pascal 里的那些想法延续并发展到 Delphi 里的？

speaker: Guest
text_en: The big thing that happened there between Turbo Pascal and Delphi was the advent of the graphical user interface, We switched from running DOS in text mode to running Windows in a GUI, and that meant a new kind of application, right, that you had to create. And at the same time, competitively, Microsoft had created Visual Basic, which was a very impressive product,
text: 在 Turbo Pascal 和 Delphi 之间，发生的最大变化就是图形用户界面的兴起。我们从在 DOS 的文本模式下运行，切换到了在 Windows 的 GUI 里运行，这就意味着你必须去创建一种全新的应用程序，对吧。与此同时，在竞争层面上，Microsoft 推出了 Visual Basic，那是个非常令人印象深刻的产品。

speaker: Guest
text_en: but still had some of the very same flaws that we knew how to compete with, in terms of interpreted versus compiled and extensible versus not or not extensible versus ours that had classes and object orientation and blah, blah, blah. First, we set out to build a Visual Basic competitor. But then we also realized that, well, that's not really enough of an angle.
text: 不过它仍然有一些和以前一样的缺陷，而这些正是我们知道该如何与之竞争的点，比如解释执行和编译执行的区别、可扩展和不可扩展的区别，以及我们的产品有类、有面向对象之类的这些东西。最开始，我们是想做一个 Visual Basic 的竞争对手。但后来我们也意识到，光靠这个角度其实还不够。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And then there was this other phenomenon that was happening at the time, which was called client-server applications. and there were a whole bunch of 4GL application development tools for database connected client server apps. And so we set out to build a tool that was like as interactive and rapid application development as Visual Basic,
text: 当时还有另一个现象也在发生，就是所谓的客户端-服务器应用。那时候有一大堆面向数据库连接的客户端-服务器应用的 4GL 开发工具。所以我们就着手去做一个工具，它要像 Visual Basic 一样具备交互性、支持快速应用开发，

speaker: Guest
text_en: but with a compiler behind it targeted also at client server enterprise apps. And that was what Delphi sort of was about, right? It worked out really well. I mean, that product to this day is still being used actively by a whole number of programmers.
text: 但背后又有一个编译器，同时也面向客户端-服务器企业应用。这大概就是 Delphi 的核心定位，对吧？结果非常成功。我的意思是，这个产品直到今天仍然有很多程序员在积极使用。

speaker: Host
text_en: I was very surprised when I worked at Skype right after Microsoft bought it. The Skype application, you probably know this, it was built in Delphi. In 2012 or 2013, there was a plan to rewrite it and move it onto something else. that rewrite midway or year in stopped. So I'm guessing that until the end of that Skype application, which was decommissioned maybe a year ago. It's amazing, isn't it?
text: 我在 Microsoft 收购 Skype 之后不久去 Skype 工作时，真的很惊讶。你大概也知道，Skype 这个应用就是用 Delphi 写的。2012 或 2013 年的时候，他们曾计划把它重写，迁移到别的东西上。但那次重写做到一半，或者做了一年左右，就停掉了。所以我猜，一直到那个 Skype 应用最终退役——大概是一年前的事——它都还在用。很神奇，不是吗？

speaker: Guest
text_en: I mean, the Delphi was and is, in some ways, a wonderful way of building Windows desktop apps. I mean, they had a great, you know, the VCL, the Visual Class Library that allowed you to inherit components and install them on the palette and make drag and drop work for your forms designers with components that you had built and whatever. It was pretty cool.
text: 我是说，Delphi 过去是、现在在某些方面也仍然是构建 Windows 桌面应用的一种很棒的方式。它有非常出色的 VCL，也就是 Visual Class Library，让你可以继承组件，把它们安装到组件面板上，还能让你自己构建的组件在窗体设计器里实现拖放，诸如此类。真的很酷。

speaker: Host
text_en: And we already heard the Microsoft link with Visual Basic. So you joined Microsoft in 1996. You worked on J++ and then later C Sharp. But can you take us back to that moment in time? What was the kind of programming environment like?
text: 我们前面已经提到了它和 Microsoft、Visual Basic 的联系。所以你在 1996 年加入了 Microsoft。你先做了 J++，后来又做了 C Sharp。不过你能带我们回到那个时间点吗？当时整体的编程环境大概是什么样的？

speaker: Guest
text_en: the environment, particularly around the time where I joined Microsoft, the mid nineties, Java had happened. Well, the browser had happened, first of all, and JavaScript. But no one really paid attention to JavaScript because that was just this little whatever thingy that was in the browser, you know, and it was slow and it was like, eh, no one uses that.
text: 当时的环境，尤其是我加入 Microsoft 的那个时期，也就是 90 年代中期，Java 已经出来了。更早其实是浏览器先火起来了，还有 JavaScript。不过那时候其实没人太把 JavaScript 当回事，因为它就是浏览器里一个小玩意儿，你懂的，而且还很慢，大家都觉得，嗯，谁会用这东西啊。

speaker: Guest
text_en: But then there was this Java thing that allowed you to create applets. Oh, my God. Applets. Fantastic. And right ones run everywhere. Yep. Yep. Everywhere. Which ran in the browser and everywhere, supposedly. And this language that was, like, simple, yet had object orientation and bytecodes and, like, was platform independent. I mean, it was, like...
text: 但后来出了 Java 这个东西，可以让你创建 applet。天哪，applet。太神了。而且是“一次编写，到处运行”。对，对，到处运行。据说既能在浏览器里跑，也能到处跑。再加上这门语言看起来很简单，但又有面向对象、字节码之类的特性，而且还是平台无关的。我是说，这简直就像……

speaker: Guest
text_en: Everyone was running around with their heads cut off thinking this was the end of languages. You know, Java is going to flatten the universe and we're all just going to be writing Java and Java applets and that's it. You know, and I actually came to Microsoft ostensibly to be the architect of Microsoft's Java development tool.
text: 所有人都慌得像无头苍蝇一样，觉得这就是编程语言的终局了。你知道，Java 要把整个宇宙都推平了，我们以后就只写 Java 和 Java applet，就这样了。其实我来 Microsoft，名义上就是来担任 Microsoft 的 Java 开发工具架构师。

speaker: Guest
text_en: and worked on Visual J++ 6.0 was the version that they had Visual J++ 1.1 at the time I joined, which was basically take Visual C++, yank out the C++ compilers, dig in a Java compiler and call it good. But it wasn't interactive. It wasn't rapid application development and whatever. And I came sort of with a whole host of knowledge of how to build interactive development tools.
text: 后来我参与做了 Visual J++ 6.0。我加入的时候，他们当时的版本是 Visual J++ 1.1，基本上就是把 Visual C++ 拿过来，把 C++ 编译器抽掉，塞进一个 Java 编译器，然后就算完事了。但它不够交互式，也谈不上快速应用开发之类的。而我当时正好带着一整套关于如何构建交互式开发工具的经验过来。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And that's what we set out to do with Visual J++ 6.0. And we also, of course, knew that, hey, you know, I mean, people are going to be running on Windows and they're going to want to be able to build Windows desktop apps. And so we built a class library that allowed you to do that. This was the precursor, WFC, I think it was called, but it was the precursor of WinForms,
text: 这也就是我们用 Visual J++ 6.0 想要去做的事。当然我们也知道，大家终究还是会跑在 Windows 上，他们也会想要构建 Windows 桌面应用。所以我们做了一个类库，让你可以这么干。那就是前身，我记得叫 WFC，它算是 WinForms 的前身。

speaker: Host
text_en: you know, in some ways. How did the development of J++ go and eventually how did it lead to the idea of like, let's do something else, completely different?
text: 某种程度上算是这样。那 J++ 的开发过程后来是怎样的？它最终又是怎么把你们引向“我们来做点别的、完全不同的东西”这个想法的？

speaker: Guest
text_en: you know, development of J++ went great until the big Sun Microsoft lawsuit got in the way. and there was, you know, and that is like, I mean, now we're talking like business and whatever. It had nothing to do with technical, but it effectively meant that Visual J++ was never going to be a product that companies would make a bet on because they full well knew that, you know, you, you're not gonna,
text: 你知道，J++ 的开发一开始非常顺利，直到后来 Sun 和 Microsoft 那场大诉讼横插一脚。然后这事儿吧，说到底已经是商业层面的事了，跟技术本身没什么关系。但它实际上意味着，Visual J++ 永远不可能成为公司愿意下注的产品，因为大家心里都很清楚，你不可能会——

speaker: Guest
text_en: you're not gonna write your app in a, in a language that has been enjoined by a judge in San Jose, you know, or, or whatever. And so we kind of realized at that point too, that maybe it's not a great strategy to, to place your, your development platform bet on, on technology that's licensed from a competitor.
text: 你不可能会用一门在 San Jose 被法官下了禁令的语言来写自己的应用，对吧，或者不管怎么说。所以我们那时候也意识到，也许把你的开发平台押注在一项从竞争对手那里授权来的技术上，并不是个特别好的策略。

speaker: Host
text_en: And that, in turn,
text: 而这反过来，

speaker: Guest
text_en: along with the sort of dev situation at the time, I mean, Microsoft's main development products at the time were in two camps. There was Visual Basic, rapid application development loved by everybody, you know, because it was so easy to build apps, But performance-wise, had problems. Extensibility-wise, wasn't so great. To write new components, you had to write them in C++ and whatever, and
text: 再加上当时那种开发环境的情况，我是说，Microsoft 当时主要的开发产品基本分成两派。一派是 Visual Basic，快速应用开发，大家都很喜欢，你懂的，因为做应用太容易了。但从性能上看，它有问题；从可扩展性上看，也不算特别好。你要写新的组件，就得用 C++ 之类的来写，然后

speaker: Guest
text_en: And then we had C++ with MFC and power and expressiveness, but really what people wanted was both. They wanted something that rolled both of those up, right? And then they also wanted modern things like garbage collection that, say, Java had, for example, an exception handling and a more object-oriented, component-oriented way of building your apps.
text: 另一派就是带 MFC 的 C++，功能强大、表达力也强，但说到底，人们真正想要的是两者兼得。他们想要一个把这两边都整合起来的东西，对吧？而且他们还想要一些现代特性，比如像 Java 那样的垃圾回收、异常处理，以及一种更面向对象、更面向组件的应用构建方式。

speaker: Host
text_en: And all of that was part of the genesis that led to .NET and to the C Sharp language. So which one was first, .NET or C Sharp inside of Microsoft? they were simultaneous,
text: 而这一切都构成了后来催生 .NET 和 C Sharp 语言的起点。所以在 Microsoft 内部，哪个先出现，.NET 还是 C Sharp？它们是同时进行的，

speaker: Guest
text_en: I would say, because we knew we wanted to build a runtime that was language independent. because we knew that we wanted to run Visual Basic on it, and we wanted a way of running C++ on it, and we wanted the ability for other languages to host themselves on this runtime. But we also knew that we needed to build a language that would appeal to both
text: 我会这么说，因为我们知道自己想构建一个与语言无关的运行时。因为我们知道，我们想让 Visual Basic 跑在上面，也想有办法让 C++ 跑在上面，还希望其他语言也能托管在这个运行时上。但我们也知道，我们需要打造一门能够同时吸引

speaker: Guest
text_en: Visual Basic and C++ users and give you sort of that golden thing in the middle, And to be frank, something that could compete with Java, right? And so that's why we...
text: Visual Basic 和 C++ 用户的语言，给你一种处在中间那个黄金平衡点的感觉。坦白说，还得是一个能和 Java 竞争的东西，对吧？所以这就是为什么我们……

speaker: Host
text_en: we started out building C-sharp. And then when you started out building C-sharp, what were your design goals? You mentioned a few things with garbage collection or exception handling, but how did you come up with like, what would this language be?
text: 我们一开始就着手构建 C-sharp。那你们刚开始做 C-sharp 的时候，设计目标是什么？你提到了垃圾回收、异常处理这些点，但你们当时是怎么想出来这门语言该是什么样子的？

speaker: Guest
text_en: like I said, I mean, the overarching thing was this power and productivity of C++ with the ease of use of visual basic, in a sense, But what it also meant was we knew we wanted to build an object-oriented language. We wanted managed code or bytecode so we could target different runtime environments. We wanted garbage collection and exception handling,
text: 就像我说的，总体目标其实是把 C++ 的能力和生产力，与 Visual Basic 的易用性结合起来，从某种意义上说就是这样。但这也意味着，我们知道自己想做一门面向对象的语言。我们想要托管代码或者字节码，这样就能面向不同的运行时环境。我们想要垃圾回收和异常处理，

speaker: Guest
text_en: but also things like a unified object system where And that's true in C-sharp. Anything can be assigned to an object. And if it's a value type, we box it. And it's a self-describing object. So reflection, you can ask an object, what are you? And you can get all of the facts about it at runtime. And you can dynamically manipulate it in ways that just don't exist in a lot of other environments.
text: 还有像统一对象系统这样的东西，这在 C-sharp 里确实是成立的。任何东西都可以赋给一个 object。如果它是值类型，我们就把它装箱。而且它还是自描述的对象。所以通过反射，你可以去问一个对象：你是什么？然后你就能在运行时拿到关于它的所有信息。你还可以用很多动态方式去操作它，而这些能力在很多别的环境里根本不存在。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And we knew we wanted to go there with that. We wanted a language that made this new model of properties, methods, and events first class because that was how components were built as opposed to just sort of functions and procedures and even objects, And then we actually also wanted to create a language that was standardized.
text: 我们当时就知道，我们想往这个方向走。我们想要一种语言，能把这种由属性、方法和事件构成的新模型变成一等公民，因为组件就是这么构建出来的，而不只是一些函数、过程，甚至不只是对象。然后我们其实还想做一门标准化的语言。

speaker: Guest
text_en: We wanted to give this language to a standardization committee and try to level the playing field there. And all of those things were sort of like what was rolled up in C-Sharp.
text: 我们想把这门语言交给标准化委员会，试着把竞争环境拉平一点。而所有这些东西，某种程度上都被打包进了 C-Sharp 里。

speaker: Host
text_en: You definitely did. C-Sharp was my first professional language where I worked with it, I think, for about five years. And I've seen both the tooling, the capabilities of language. And I still think to this date, in many ways, that old version of C-Sharp was ahead of some languages today in some ways. So it's very interesting to see how rich that language was when it came out.
text: 你们确实做到了。C-Sharp 是我第一门真正用于职业工作的语言，我记得我大概用了五年。我见识过它的工具链，也见识过这门语言本身的能力。直到今天我依然觉得，从很多方面看，那个老版本的 C-Sharp 在某些地方都领先于今天的一些语言。所以回头看，这门语言刚发布时就已经这么丰富，真的很有意思。

speaker: Host
text_en: And of course, the developer love that followed. But can you take us back? What did it take to build a language like this? Just in the more, again, software engineers, people were listening. They're used to building the SaaS apps, backend services, you know, like certain projects. but we are not familiar with what it takes to build a language,
text: 当然，后来开发者对它的喜爱也随之而来。但你能带我们回到当时吗？打造这样一门语言到底需要什么？还是从更偏软件工程的角度来说吧，很多在听的人平时做的是 SaaS 应用、后端服务之类的项目，但我们并不熟悉做一门语言到底意味着什么。

speaker: Host
text_en: especially something with such large ambitions inside Microsoft. We knew millions of developers ideally would be using it. How did you get to this? How did you come up with the roadmap? How big or large or small team needs to work on this?
text: 尤其是在 Microsoft 内部做一个野心这么大的东西。我们知道，理想情况下会有几百万开发者来用它。你们是怎么走到这一步的？路线图是怎么定出来的？需要多大、或者说多小的团队来做这件事？

speaker: Guest
text_en: I think early on, we decided that we want to have a team of people design this language, not just one. I was sort of the guy who ran the group of designers. But we put together a group of six people or so, six, seven people. And we got in a room three times a week for two hours and just started the design, you know, like literally let's start from the top. What is the, we all knew what, I mean,
text: 我觉得一开始我们就决定，这门语言要由一个团队来设计，而不是靠某一个人。我算是负责带这个设计团队的人。我们组了一个大概六个人左右的小组，六七个人吧。然后我们每周三次，把大家叫到一个房间里，每次两个小时，就这样开始做设计。真的是从最顶层开始：我们先从头来想，这到底是什么。我们其实都知道，我是说，

speaker: Guest
text_en: these were all people who had built or worked on programming languages before. And, and had seen all of the things you're supposed to do and all the things you're not supposed to do. And quite honestly, language design is. 90% the same and 10% new for pretty much every language. Every language you build still has to have a compiler. Compiler is still built pretty much the same way.
text: 这些人以前都做过编程语言，或者参与过编程语言的开发。哪些事情该做，哪些事情不该做，他们都见过。老实说，语言设计这件事，对几乎每一门语言来说，90% 都是相同的，只有 10% 是新的。你做任何一门语言，还是得有编译器。而编译器的构建方式，基本上也都差不多。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And of course, as time marched on, people demand more and more. You have to have IDEs, you have to have frameworks, you have to blah, blah, blah, blah, blah. So there's a lot of experience you want to pull in. And there's a lot of work that you're doing that isn't really per se new. But every time around you try to fix the problems that you've been exposed to This
text: 当然，随着时间推移，人们的要求也越来越多。你得有 IDE，得有框架，还得有各种这个那个。所以你会想把很多已有的经验吸收进来。而且你做的很多工作，严格来说也不算全新。但每一轮你都会试着去修复那些你已经暴露出来、遇到过的问题。这个——

speaker: Guest
text_en: language design group worked together for years on end And it was lovely to come in to work with a new idea And then immediately have five or six people that you could sit down and have a deep discussion with Without first having to spend an hour level setting Do you know what I mean? Yeah, yeah And that worked really, really well Because we could just jump right in
text: 这个语言设计小组一起合作了很多年。能带着一个新想法来上班，然后立刻就有五六个人可以坐下来深入讨论，这种感觉特别棒。而且不用先花一个小时把大家拉到同一个认知水平上。你懂我意思吧？对，对。这个方式效果真的非常非常好，因为我们可以直接切入正题。

speaker: Guest
text_en: and have two hours of technical discussion. And everyone was cognizant of, if someone comes up with a new idea, now it's our job to try to shoot it down. What's wrong with this idea? Do you know what I mean? And if it could go, if it could stand the test of that, then it was probably a decent idea. And so that was kind of how we ran the design. And then I wrote
text: 然后一聊就是两个小时的技术讨论。大家也都很清楚，如果有人提出了一个新想法，那接下来我们的任务就是尽量去挑它的毛病。这个想法哪里有问题？你懂我意思吧？如果它能经得住这样的考验，那它大概率就是个不错的想法。所以我们当时基本就是这么做设计的。然后我一边

speaker: Guest
text_en: the specification of the language in parallel with our design meetings. And then we had a group that was in parallel implementing the compiler in actually implementing it in C++ or rather C++ because we didn't use all of the C++ features, you know, in that compiler implementation. But it wasn't until the Rosslyn project that we self-hosted the C-sharp compiler.
text: 配合设计会议，一边写这门语言的规范。同时还有另一个小组并行在实现编译器，实际是用 C++ 来实现的，或者更准确地说，是“某种程度上的 C++”，因为我们在那个编译器实现里并没有用到 C++ 的全部特性。不过一直到 Rosslyn 项目，我们才让 C-sharp 编译器实现了自举。

speaker: Host
text_en: And Rosslyn meaning that the compiler is in C-sharp, right?
text: 所以 Rosslyn 的意思是，编译器是用 C-sharp 写的，对吧？

speaker: Guest
text_en: a project that came later to build the compiler in itself. And also early on,
text: 对，那是后来的一个项目，用这门语言自己来构建它自己的编译器。另外在早期，

speaker: Host
text_en: you know, this is,
text: 你知道，这个——

speaker: Guest
text_en: you got to remember back then, IDEs were not really all that fancy. You know, I mean, we have syntax colorization. Statement completion was kind of like, some IDEs were starting to dabble in it, but it wasn't really a norm. So we built like, in a sense, a classic compiler. But then we also built this like mini language servicey thing that sort of cut some corners and whatever,
text: 你得记得，在那个年代，IDE 其实还没那么花哨。我的意思是，我们有语法高亮。语句补全算是有点那个意思了，有些 IDE 开始尝试做，但还远远不是标配。所以我们做出来的，从某种意义上说，是一个很经典的编译器。但同时我们也做了一个有点像迷你语言服务的东西，算是做了一些取舍、走了一些捷径之类的。

speaker: Guest
text_en: but could do some rudimentary statement completion and syntax coloring. But in a sense, we had two implementations that we had to evolve in parallel. And over time, that became quite a drag, right? text: 但它能做一些很基础的语句补全和语法高亮。不过从某种意义上说，我们当时有两套实现，必须并行演进。时间一长，这就变成了很大的负担，对吧？因为随着我们加入 generics、再加上其他功能、链接之类各种东西，就会觉得，天啊，现在这变成了——我们得把所有这些功能都实现两遍。

speaker: Guest
text_en: in the real compiler and in the language service, right? And so that ultimately led us to this project called Roslyn, where we built a single compiler that really is both, it's a compiler that can both function as a command line compiler and as an interactive service inside the IDE. TypeScript is built that same way also,
text: 一遍在真正的编译器里，一遍在语言服务里，对吧？所以这最终促成了一个叫 Roslyn 的项目，我们构建了一个统一的编译器，它实际上两者都是：既能作为命令行编译器运行，也能作为 IDE 里的交互式服务运行。TypeScript 也是用同样的方式构建的，

speaker: Host
text_en: There are many useful things not being taught in school, even when they are useful to learn about. One of these really useful tools that I must mention is our seasonal sponsor, TurboPuffer. TurboPuffer is a ridiculously scalable, fast and cheap vector and full-text search engine built by an engineering team that I really like.
text: 学校里没教、但其实很值得学的东西有很多。说到这里，我必须提一下我们这一季的赞助商 TurboPuffer。TurboPuffer 是一个扩展性强得离谱、速度快、成本低的向量和全文搜索引擎，背后的工程团队也是我非常喜欢的一群人。

speaker: Host
text_en: The first time I heard about them was when I was talking with one of Cursor's co-founders about how their vector database could not keep up with the number of code bases that they were adding. This was back in 2023. Cursor did something seemingly risky. They took a bet on what was little known and relative to the new product at the time, Turbo Puffer. But it paid off.
text: 我第一次听说他们，是在和 Cursor 的一位联合创始人聊天时。当时他们在说，自己的向量数据库已经跟不上不断新增的代码库数量了。那还是 2023 年。Cursor 做了一件看起来有点冒险的事：他们押注了当时还没什么人知道、而且相对还是个新产品的 Turbo Puffer。但结果证明，这个决定是对的。

speaker: Host
text_en: Cursor moved their semantic search workload over and Turbo Puffer was indeed able to handle Cursor's massive, ever-increasing load. The reason has everything to do with smart engineering. Turbo Puffer is built on top of object storage with smart caching on NVMe SSDs. Cursor's active code bases get loaded into the cache so searches are fast and inactive code bases fade into object storage.
text: Cursor 把他们的语义搜索负载迁了过去，而 Turbo Puffer 的确扛住了 Cursor 庞大且持续增长的负载。原因完全在于聪明的工程设计。Turbo Puffer 构建在对象存储之上，并在 NVMe SSD 上做了智能缓存。Cursor 的活跃代码库会被加载进缓存里，所以搜索速度很快；不活跃的代码库则会逐渐回落到对象存储中。

speaker: Host
text_en: Cursor has so many There are many good things to say about TurboPuffer. They cut their semantic search costs by 95% when they switched, they think of TurboPuffer engineers as an immediate extension of their team in Slack, and TurboPuffer is one of the few pieces of infrastructure that they have not had to worry about as they scaled.
text: 关于 TurboPuffer，Cursor 有很多好话可说。他们切换过去之后，语义搜索成本降低了 95%；他们把 TurboPuffer 的工程师视作自己团队在 Slack 里的即时延伸；而且随着业务扩张，TurboPuffer 也是他们少数几个几乎不用操心的基础设施之一。

speaker: Host
text_en: TurboPuffer indexes over 4 trillion documents for vector and full-text search and is used by the likes of Antrophic, Notion, Linear, Ramp, and many others. I'm getting to know the TurboPuffer team and will share more about some of the cool things that they do behind the scenes throughout the season. If you need vector search or full-text search at scale, think TurboPuffer.
text: TurboPuffer 为向量和全文搜索索引了超过 4 万亿份文档，Anthropic、Notion、Linear、Ramp 等很多公司都在使用它。我最近也在逐渐了解 TurboPuffer 团队，这一季里我还会继续分享一些他们幕后做的很酷的事情。如果你需要大规模的向量搜索或全文搜索，就想想 TurboPuffer。

text_en: Of course, you had the, you already said the group criticized it. Did you have an internal beta testers? But this is not your average software service for sure.
