They had sort of used a hacked up version of C++ to implement, which was kind of odd because, I mean, it was like targeting bytecodes, but not really. And so they switched to C Sharp and that helped a lot. And then we had other internal teams using it.
text: 对，幸运的是我们有内部客户。.NET framework 很快就开始用 C Sharp 来实现了。他们之前其实是用一个魔改版的 C++ 来实现的，这就有点奇怪，因为它算是在面向字节码，但又不完全是。所以后来他们切换到了 C Sharp，这帮了很大忙。然后我们还有其他内部团队也在用它。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And so we got a bunch of feedback that way. And then we had, you know, the cycle was not that long, right? I mean, I think we started in late 98 and by the PDC of 2000, we had, we
text: 所以我们通过这种方式拿到了很多反馈。而且我们的迭代周期也没那么长，对吧？我是说，我记得我们是 98 年底开始的，到 2000 年的 PDC 时，我们已经，我们

speaker: Guest
text_en: We basically gave away beta copies and got tons of users onto it. Now,
text: 基本上已经把 beta 版本免费发出去了，也因此吸引了大量用户。现在，

speaker: Host
text_en: C Sharp introduced a lot of new features that were net new, I think, to programming languages. Link is certainly one of them. But one thing that might have been maybe one of the most influential parts that other languages adopted as inspiration was the async await. setup. Looking back, what do you think you got right with this design and why did it become so copyable
text: 我觉得 C Sharp 引入了很多对编程语言来说全新的特性。LINQ 肯定算其中之一。但有一项后来可能成了影响力最大的设计之一，也被很多其他语言借鉴了，那就是 async/await 这套机制。回头看，你觉得这个设计哪些地方做对了？为什么它会这么容易被

speaker: Host
text_en: across languages like JavaScript, Python, Rust, and others?
text: JavaScript、Python、Rust 等语言广泛借鉴？

speaker: Guest
text_en: a lot of languages are built around cooperative multitasking in the sense that they have an event loop that sits and dispatches events and then, you know, you handle the event and then you yield back to event handler loop, and it all runs in a single thread cooperatively, right? The problem with that is if you then want to do some long-running work,
text: 很多语言都是围绕协作式多任务构建的，也就是说它们有一个事件循环，坐在那里分发事件，然后你去处理这个事件，处理完再把控制权交还给事件处理循环，整个过程都在单线程里以协作的方式运行，对吧？这里的问题是，如果你接下来想做一些长时间运行的工作，

speaker: Guest
text_en: how do I stop in the middle of this piece of long-running work and yield back to the event loop cooperatively, And then when my result is ready, then I can come back and continue executing here. Well, in order to do that in in an inverted architecture like that, you have to build a state machine. And state machines are notoriously hard for people to implement because you've got
text: 那我要怎么在这段长时间运行的工作做到一半时停下来，协作式地把控制权交还给事件循环？然后等结果准备好了，我再回来从这里继续执行。要做到这一点，在这种反转的架构里，你就得构建一个状态机。而状态机出了名地难写，因为你得

speaker: Guest
text_en: to move all of your state off of the stack into objects. You've got to remember where, and then you have this big case statement that envelopes your entire logic. And it's like, it's a nightmare to figure out, right? But the transformation from serially executing code into a state machine that's this continuation processing
text: 把所有状态都从栈上挪到对象里。你还得记住执行到哪里了，然后还会有一个巨大的 case 语句把你的整套逻辑都包起来。总之，这东西理起来简直是噩梦，对吧？不过，把顺序执行的代码转换成状态机，也就是这种 continuation processing

speaker: Guest
text_en: style translation is actually one that you can do in a machine-based fashion. You can have the compiler write the state machine if you introduce syntax that allows you to indicate where you want to yield. And that's what await is. Await is basically, I'm saying, I want to yield here, and I want to yield this promise.
text: 风格的转换，其实是可以用机器化的方式来做的。如果你引入一种语法，让你标明自己想在哪些地方 yield，那就可以让编译器来帮你写这个状态机。而这就是 await。await 本质上就是在说：我想在这里 yield，我想把这个 promise yield 出去。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And then when the promise completes, I want you to come back here and continue executing. And then the compiler writes a state machine around it, and it actually turns it into this big switch statement. and moves all of the state that survives across the await into something that's heap allocated so it can be brought back. And doing all of that work is something that compilers are great at.
text: 然后等这个 promise 完成之后，我希望你再回到这里继续执行。接着编译器就会围绕它生成一个状态机，实际上会把它变成一个很大的 switch 语句，并把那些跨过 await 之后还需要保留的状态，挪到堆上分配的地方，这样之后才能再取回来。而做这些工作，正是编译器最擅长的。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And so that was sort of the idea that We have this new style of programming where we're using promises or the equivalent of promises and the ability to yield. And then we have callbacks. But trying to write your program in that style, that's also what JavaScript suffer from a lot, It's like all this callback style stuff. And with async await,
text: 所以当时大概的思路就是：我们有了这种新的编程风格，使用 promise 或者类似 promise 的东西，再加上 yield 的能力。然后我们还有回调。但如果真的按这种风格去写程序，这也是 JavaScript 吃了很多苦头的地方，就是到处都是这种 callback 风格的代码。而有了 async await，

speaker: Guest
text_en: you get sort of the illusion that you're just writing normal sequential code. And then the compiler does the painful transformation for you. And that turns out to be really useful. Now, arguably an alternative way of doing this is to use threads in the OS. But the problem with threads is that they come with preemptiveness and the OS has the ability to preempt you at any point in time.
text: 你会有一种错觉，好像自己只是在写普通的顺序代码。然后那些痛苦的转换都由编译器替你完成了。事实证明这非常有用。当然，也可以说另一种做法是直接使用操作系统里的线程。但线程的问题在于它带有抢占性，操作系统可以在任何时刻抢占你。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And that's not necessarily what you want. And your UI, now you have to be multi-threaded in your UI and all sorts of other problems come along with it. Plus threads are heavyweight. and typically not well-suited for lightweight tasks like you could do with async functions. So there are pros and cons. Async and wait introduces this notion of function coloring, which is unfortunate,
text: 而这未必是你想要的。这样一来，你的 UI 也得变成多线程的，还会连带冒出各种别的问题。而且线程很重，通常并不适合 async function 这种轻量级任务。所以这里面是有利有弊的。Async 和 await 还引入了所谓的 function coloring，这一点挺遗憾的，

speaker: Guest
text_en: where you have two kinds of functions, async functions and regular functions. And all the red functions can call the blue functions, but the blue functions can't call the red functions. And so that means once you want a red function, Now everything above it has to be red. If you want from a sync function to call something async,
text: 也就是你有两类函数：async 函数和普通函数。所有红色函数都可以调用蓝色函数，但蓝色函数不能调用红色函数。所以这就意味着，一旦你想要一个红色函数，那么它上面的所有东西都得变成红色。如果你想让一个同步函数去调用某个异步的东西，

speaker: Guest
text_en: then you've got to turn this function into an async function and its caller has to be, etc., etc., So that's unfortunate. And that's why some environments like Go, for example, has Go routines and green threads, which are really language-emulated lightweight threads. that kind of do what I'm talking about, but at a much lower cost. But you avoid the function coloring.
text: 那你就得把这个函数改成 async 函数，而调用它的函数也得跟着改，诸如此类，一层层传上去。所以这挺不理想的。这也是为什么有些环境，比如 Go，会有 Go routines 和 green threads，本质上它们就是语言模拟出来的轻量级线程。它们某种程度上能做到我说的这种效果，但成本低得多。而且还能避免这种“函数染色”问题。

speaker: Guest
text_en: So there is a bunch of different But for an environment that already exists like JavaScript or like C Sharp and the Windows event loop and whatever,
text: 所以其实有很多不同的办法。不过对于像 JavaScript，或者 C Sharp 加上 Windows 事件循环这类已经存在的环境来说，

speaker: Host
text_en: this was the right solution. Speaking of JavaScript, as C Sharp was becoming really popular across startups, enterprises, and so on, it was exploding in popular games as well. To this day, it's very popular with games development. But JavaScript was starting to become more popular. Can you take us back to... your observations on how JavaScript went from the mid-90s to this script language
text: 这就是正确的解决方案。说到 JavaScript，随着 C Sharp 在创业公司、企业等各种场景里变得非常流行，它在游戏领域里也迅速火了起来。直到今天，它在游戏开发里依然很受欢迎。但那时候 JavaScript 也开始越来越流行了。你能带我们回顾一下吗……说说你观察到的 JavaScript 是怎么从 90 年代中期那种

speaker: Host
text_en: no one really took seriously to just exploding in popularity.
text: 没人真正当回事的脚本语言，一路爆发式流行起来的？

speaker: Guest
text_en: I think it was sort of a confluence of a number of things that happened in the early 2000s, First of all, the JavaScript platform, execution platform, matured a lot. Like Google did their excellent work on V8 and all of a sudden make JavaScript a fairly performant programming language. HTML5 got ratified and we were getting to a point now where you could actually build real UIs in JavaScript.
text: 我觉得这有点像是 2000 年代初发生的好几件事汇合到了一起。首先，JavaScript 平台，也就是它的执行平台，成熟了很多。比如 Google 在 V8 上做了非常出色的工作，一下子让 JavaScript 变成了一门性能相当不错的编程语言。再加上 HTML5 完成了标准化，我们也开始走到这样一个阶段：你真的可以用 JavaScript 构建像样的真实 UI 了。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And there was this device revolution that the iPhone set off and And all of a sudden, we have all of these different form factors. It's not just Windows PCs on the desktop anymore. It's all sorts of diverse devices. But lo and behold, they all run browsers with JavaScript in. Lo and behold, the real cross-platform language isn't Java. It's JavaScript.
text: 然后还有 iPhone 引爆的那场设备革命。突然之间，我们有了各种各样不同的设备形态。不再只是桌面上的 Windows PC 了，而是各种各样的设备。但说来也巧，它们全都运行带有 JavaScript 的浏览器。结果真正的跨平台语言不是 Java，而是 JavaScript。

speaker: Host
text_en: Who would have thought?
text: 谁能想到呢？

speaker: Guest
text_en: And so the world started opening its eyes to that and started building larger and larger applications in JavaScript. And we saw that externally, but also internally. And one of the trigger events was when the Outlook.com team came to the C Sharp team and asked us whether we would pretty please productize this thing called ScriptSharp. And we go, well, what is ScriptSharp?
text: 于是整个世界开始意识到这一点，开始用 JavaScript 构建越来越大型的应用。我们在外部看到了这种趋势，内部也是如此。其中一个触发事件是，Outlook.com 团队找到 C Sharp 团队，问我们能不能行行好，把一个叫 ScriptSharp 的东西产品化。我们当时就说，呃，ScriptSharp 是什么？

speaker: Guest
text_en: It's this cross-compiler that allows you to cross-compile C-sharp into JavaScript, such that you can basically treat JavaScript as an instruction language and run your C-sharp apps in a browser. And I'm like, well, why would anyone want to do that? Well, because then you can get a grown-up programming language with grown-up tooling. You can use Visual Studio. You can have projects.
text: 它是一个交叉编译器，可以把 C-sharp 交叉编译成 JavaScript，这样你基本上就可以把 JavaScript 当成一种指令语言，在浏览器里运行你的 C-sharp 应用。我当时就想，为什么会有人想这么干？他们说，因为这样你就能用上一门成熟的编程语言和成熟的工具链。你可以用 Visual Studio，可以有项目。

speaker: Guest
text_en: You can do all of these wonderful things, you know, that you can't do with JavaScript because JavaScript is just a scripting language with shitty tooling. And we were like, wow, really? Well, gosh, well, perhaps a better approach would be to fix JavaScript. I mean, surely you're not going to be best of breed in the JavaScript ecosystem by telling
text: 你还能做所有这些很棒的事情，而这些是你在 JavaScript 里做不到的，因为 JavaScript 说到底就是一门脚本语言，工具链还很烂。我们当时就觉得，哇，真的吗？那也许更好的办法是把 JavaScript 修好。我的意思是，你总不可能靠告诉大家去写另一门编程语言，

speaker: Guest
text_en: people to write in a different programming language. Although plenty of people were like, remember CoffeeScript and all of these other like languages that targeted JavaScript,
text: 就在 JavaScript 生态里做到最强吧。虽然当时确实很多人都在这么干，比如还记得 CoffeeScript 吗，还有其他那些以 JavaScript 为目标的语言，

speaker: Host
text_en: So they were a programming language, right? Which generated JavaScript, but it wasn't JavaScript itself.
text: 所以它们本身是编程语言，对吧？会生成 JavaScript，但它本身并不是 JavaScript。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Yes, they were super popular. I mean, like so many different things did that. But JavaScript is actually a pretty decent little language. There are just some things missing. You got to give credit there to Brendan Eich. I mean, he understood functional programming. And Brendan Eich, the creator of JavaScript. And he got functions as first class objects right in JavaScript,
text: 对，它们当时特别火。真的，有很多很多东西都是这么做的。不过 JavaScript 其实本身是一门相当不错的小语言，只是缺了些东西。这点得给 Brendan Eich 记一功。Brendan Eich 就是 JavaScript 的创造者。他在 JavaScript 里把函数作为一等对象这件事做对了，

speaker: Guest
text_en: which is godsend and beautiful, but it doesn't have a type system. And we knew from experience that you cannot build good tooling without a type system. You can build decent tooling, but it's never going to scale. It's never going to scale to large teams because you can't describe your intents in the code. There's no way of formalizing any of this stuff and there's no way of analyzing it
text: 这简直是天赐之物，也非常优美，但它没有类型系统。而我们从经验中知道，没有类型系统，你就做不出好的工具。你可以做出还不错的工具，但永远没法扩展。它没法扩展到大型团队，因为你没法在代码里表达你的意图。没有办法把这些东西形式化，也没有办法去分析它，

speaker: Guest
text_en: and there's no way of using it. in an IDE to give you statement completion and refactoring and go to definition and find all references and blah, blah, blah, blah, all of that stuff, That germinated the idea of, hey, it's, we can, we could create a superset of JavaScript that adds a type system and then we could just compile it away. But then we have,
text: 也没法在 IDE 里利用这些信息，给你语句补全、重构、跳转到定义、查找所有引用之类的，诸如此类这些功能。于是这个想法就慢慢萌生了：嘿，我们可以做一个 JavaScript 的超集，给它加上类型系统，然后再把这些东西编译掉。但接着我们又遇到了，

speaker: Guest
text_en: now we have the foundation for great tooling and then we could build a great tooling on top and actually create a wonderful development experience, right?
text: 现在我们有了打造优秀工具链的基础，然后就能在这之上做出很棒的工具，真正创造出极佳的开发体验，对吧？

speaker: Host
text_en: That was sort of like what we set out to do. When you set out to do this, you not only set out to do this, but you set out for some reason to do it as open source, which took everyone outside of Microsoft as a surprise because old Microsoft under Steve Ballmer was notoriously perceived as anti-open source back then with Windows and C Sharp back in the day, of course, I'm talking about.
text: 这差不多就是我们当初想做的事。你们开始做这件事的时候，不只是决定要做它，还出于某种原因决定把它做成开源，这让 Microsoft 之外的所有人都很意外。因为以前 Steve Ballmer 时代的老 Microsoft，大家普遍都觉得它是反开源的，尤其是当年 Windows 和 C Sharp 那个时期，我说的是那个时候。

speaker: Guest
text_en: You know, Microsoft was slowly waking up to the fact that open source was not going to go away, and open source was where developers wanted to be, and they were voting with their feet. Yet, there's a collective DNA, you know, that has been trained to pull you in the other direction, And so that battle was, we were right in the center of that.
text: 你知道，Microsoft 当时在慢慢意识到一个事实：开源不会消失，而且开发者想待的地方就是开源，他们已经在用脚投票了。但与此同时，公司内部也有一种集体 DNA，一直被训练着把你往相反的方向拉。所以那场拉扯，我们当时正处在最中心。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And we full well knew that there was absolutely zero chance that we would appeal to the JavaScript ecosystem with a proprietary programming language license from Microsoft. No, no one was going to come. It had to be open source. There was just no two ways about it, right? But getting that off the ground inside Microsoft, it took some pulling.
text: 而且我们非常清楚，如果拿着一个来自 Microsoft 的专有编程语言许可证去面对 JavaScript 生态，那是绝对不可能打动他们的。不，不会有人来的。它必须是开源，这根本没有第二种可能，对吧？但要在 Microsoft 内部把这件事真正推动起来，还是得费不少力气。

speaker: Guest
text_en: and we paid some taxes. We did eventually get the okay to do open source because we had two technical fellows, myself and Steve Luco, who was the other co-inventor of TypeScript, insisting that that was what we had to do. And so, okay, people weren't going to debate that, but But of course you have to pay the tax and be on Microsoft's open source repository called CodePlex,
text: 我们也付出了一些代价。最后我们确实拿到了开源的批准，因为有两位 technical fellow——我自己，还有 TypeScript 的另一位共同发明者 Steve Luco——一直坚持这就是我们必须做的事。所以，好吧，大家也就不再争论了。但当然，你得交这个“税”，也就是必须放到 Microsoft 的开源代码仓库 CodePlex 上，

speaker: Guest
text_en: where exactly no one was. And so we were there for the first two years and it kind of was crickets, you know. And it wasn't until 2014 when we moved on to GitHub that things really started to get moving. with adoption. And also, honestly, it totally changed our workflow. You know, there's open source and there's open development.
text: 而那个地方根本没人。所以最开始两年我们都在那里，基本上就是一片寂静，没什么动静。直到 2014 年我们迁到 GitHub，采用情况才真正开始起飞。而且说实话，那也彻底改变了我们的工作流。你知道，开源和开放式开发其实不是一回事。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And we were technically open source in the beginning, but it was not open development. We would sort of lop the source code out in its repository and scrape the issues off of that and put it into our internal issue tracker and then, you know. But once we switched to GitHub, the entire workflow moved to open development also.
text: 我们一开始在技术上确实算是开源了，但并不是开放式开发。我们大概就是把源码扔到仓库里，再把上面的 issue 抓下来，转到我们内部的 issue tracker 里去处理，诸如此类。但一旦切到 GitHub，整个工作流也都转成了开放式开发。

speaker: Guest
text_en: and that I love that workflow and that's we've been there now for over a decade and it's been fantastic and it's what made the product as good as it is just over a
text: 而我很喜欢那套工作流，我们到现在已经在那里待了十多年了，效果非常棒，也正是它让这个产品能做到今天这么好，某种程度上就是因为这个。

speaker: Host
text_en: decade later so the language moves to GitHub in 2014 and in November 2025 The GitHub Octoverse report revealed that TypeScript became the most popular language across GitHub. Outside of the type system, what do you think made TypeScript this popular? And of course, we've had other languages, Python being the other very popular one, but what captures developers' preferences this well?
text: 十年之后，这门语言在 2014 年迁移到了 GitHub，而在 2025 年 11 月，GitHub 的 Octoverse 报告显示 TypeScript 成了 GitHub 上最受欢迎的语言。先不谈类型系统，你觉得 TypeScript 为什么会这么受欢迎？当然，我们也有其他语言，Python 也是另一门非常热门的语言，但到底是什么这么精准地抓住了开发者的偏好？

speaker: Guest
text_en: Well, I think it didn't just happen overnight. And if you look back at that, all of a sudden we surfaced as number 10, and then we climbed slowly over the years up and sat next to JavaScript, right? And of course, if you added JavaScript and TypeScript together, then we were already number one. It's just, which syntax? Were you using type annotations or not?
text: 我觉得这并不是一夜之间发生的。你回头看，会发现我们一开始突然出现在第 10 名，然后这些年一路慢慢往上爬，最后和 JavaScript 并列在一起，对吧？当然，如果把 JavaScript 和 TypeScript 算在一起，那我们其实早就是第一了。区别只在于你用的是哪种语法：你有没有写类型标注？

speaker: Guest
text_en: And more and more people over time just decided to adopt that. I mean, some early on were using JS doc or whatever, you know, like these types in comments or that we also supported. But gradually, I think people just realized, hey, this is this is the right way to do it. And the reason they came, I think, is absolutely because of the better tooling.
text: 而且随着时间推移，越来越多人决定采用它。我的意思是，早些时候有些人会用 JSDoc 之类的东西，你知道，就是把类型写在注释里，这种方式我们也支持。但慢慢地，我觉得大家就是意识到，嗯，这才是正确的做法。而他们之所以转过来，我认为绝对是因为更好的工具支持。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And I think we were totally right there that like adding an erasable type system and then using that to enable great tooling. is really where the programmer productivity boost is realized.
text: 而且我觉得我们当时的判断完全是对的：加入一个可擦除的类型系统，再用它来实现强大的工具支持，这才是真正带来程序员生产力提升的地方。

speaker: Host
text_en: And I guess this is where we cannot not mention VS Code, which shipped that great tooling also as a free to use for most people, or at least initially for most people, which also made a big difference.
text: 我想这里就不能不提 VS Code 了，它把那套很棒的工具支持也带给了大家，而且对大多数人来说是免费可用的，至少一开始对大多数人是这样，这也带来了很大的不同。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Yeah, that's our sister project, which is written in TypeScript. And so they were one of our earliest adopters, and we work pretty closely with them to this day. That whole interplay, that in turn is also what led to the invention of LSP, the language server protocol that now pretty much every tool vendor uses to enable interactive services in the IDE. Oddly,
text: 对，那是我们的姊妹项目，也是用 TypeScript 写的。所以他们是我们最早的一批采用者之一，而且直到今天我们都和他们合作得非常紧密。整个这种相互促进的关系，后来也直接催生了 LSP，也就是 language server protocol。现在几乎所有工具厂商都用它来在 IDE 里提供交互式服务。说来也挺有意思的，

speaker: Guest
text_en: it isn't until this port to go now that we're switching to LSP, we had our own precursor of LSP because LSP didn't exist when we first integrated TypeScript into Visual Studio Code. But there were a lot of learnings from that. So it's been an incredibly symbiotic and fulfilling experience to build these two projects in parallel in open source.
text: 直到现在做这个 Go 版本移植时，我们才开始切换到 LSP；因为当年我们第一次把 TypeScript 集成进 Visual Studio Code 的时候，LSP 还根本不存在。所以那时候我们有自己的一套、算是 LSP 前身的方案。不过我们也从中学到了很多东西。所以能在开源里并行打造这两个项目，一直都是一种非常互相成就、也非常有满足感的经历。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And I think it has totally changed people's view of Microsoft in the developer ecosystem.
text: 而且我觉得，这也彻底改变了大家在开发者生态里对 Microsoft 的看法。

speaker: Host
text_en: For us developers who, again, are not as familiar with compilers themselves. Of course, I use TypeScript and I'm aware that there's some compilation going. Could you give us a brief overview of what the types of compiler pipeline looks like in terms of parts and what parts you specifically focus on more?
text: 对于我们这些开发者来说，还是不太熟悉编译器本身。当然，我会用 TypeScript，也知道这里面会有一些编译过程。你能不能先简单给我们概括一下，一个编译器流水线通常由哪些部分组成，以及你们具体更关注哪些部分？

speaker: Guest
text_en: It's in many ways a fairly typical compiler and in many ways not. Pretty much every compiler has you know, what's known as a lexer or a scanner that takes text and turns it into tokens. And then typically on top of that, you have a parser that takes the tokens, checks their sequencing, and then makes abstract syntax trees, which is a, you know,
text: 它在很多方面都是一个相当典型的编译器，但在很多方面又不太一样。基本上每个编译器都会有一个所谓的 lexer 或 scanner，它会把文本转换成 token。然后通常在这之上会有一个 parser，它接收这些 token，检查它们的排列顺序，然后生成抽象语法树，也就是一个，怎么说呢，

speaker: Guest
text_en: a tree that you can navigate that effectively is a map of the source code, you know, but broken into syntactic primitives and checked that, you know, like syntactically everything is, or grammatically that everything is correct. So those are the first two stages of the pipeline. Then we have, we have one extra pass that we call the binder, which is, you know, once we have the parse trees,
text: 一个你可以遍历的树状结构，本质上可以看作是源代码的一张映射图，只不过它被拆分成了语法层面的基本单元，并且会检查语法上、或者说文法上，一切是不是都正确。所以这是流水线的前两个阶段。接下来我们还有一个额外的 pass，我们叫它 binder。也就是在我们拿到语法树之后，

speaker: Guest
text_en: then we bind symbol information to them where we find all of the declarations of variables and whatever, and build symbol tables and attach them to their functions such that we can then later look up names effectively. And we also build in the binder, we build a control flow graph, and I can talk about what that helps us do. And then we have the type checker.
text: 我们会把符号信息绑定到这些树上，找出所有变量之类的声明，构建符号表，并把它们附加到对应的函数上，这样之后我们就能高效地查找名称了。我们还会在 binder 里构建控制流图，我也可以讲讲这能帮助我们做什么。再往后就是 type checker。

speaker: Guest
text_en: which is the largest part of our pipeline. And that's the thing that checks semantically that your program is correct. It's the thing that figures out types and checks that the types relate correctly and that you're assigning the right thing to the right thing. And then that, you know, that you're calling something that actually exists and so forth.
text: 它是我们整个流水线里最大的一部分。它负责从语义层面检查你的程序是不是正确。它会推导类型，检查类型之间的关系是否正确，检查你是不是把正确的东西赋给了正确的对象。然后还会检查你调用的东西是不是真的存在，诸如此类。

speaker: Host
text_en: And then we have an optional stage at the end called our emitter.
text: 最后我们还有一个可选阶段，叫 emitter。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And normally the emitter infrastructure in a compiler is also quite big because that's where you go from intermediate representation to machine code or bytecode. Now, in our case, we just erase types, if you will. Well, we kind of do two things in our compiler. Actually, early on, it was very much about A erasing the types, but B also down leveling your code.
text: 通常来说，编译器里的 emitter 基础设施也会相当庞大，因为那是把中间表示转换成机器码或者字节码的地方。但在我们的场景里，你可以理解为我们只是把类型擦除掉。嗯，我们的编译器其实大致做两件事。早期主要就是 A，把类型擦掉；B，把你的代码降级处理。

speaker: Guest
text_en: So we would take newer ECMAScript features that weren't yet supported by the runtimes, for example, classes, and then we would down level them to constructor functions and whatever. And so we would rewrite the code. And that was a very popular feature early on. Now, pretty much every browser is evergreen and, you know, like ECMAScript features are caught up. And so that's not as important anymore.
text: 也就是说，我们会把一些运行时环境当时还不支持的较新的 ECMAScript 特性，比如 class，把它们降级成构造函数之类的形式。所以我们会重写代码。这个功能在早期非常受欢迎。现在基本上所有浏览器都是 evergreen 的了，而且 ECMAScript 特性支持也都跟上来了，所以这件事现在就没那么重要了。

speaker: Guest
text_en: So our emitter is effectively, you know, a thing that just erases type annotations and spits out the JavaScript code that can run unannotated and also can spit out declaration files, which are summaries of your modules and so forth. But those are sort of the stages. Now, the thing that's interesting about the compiler, though,
text: 所以我们的 emitter 本质上就是一个把类型注解擦掉、吐出可以在没有注解的情况下运行的 JavaScript 代码的东西，同时它也能生成 declaration files，也就是模块之类内容的摘要。不过这些都算是几个阶段。真正有意思的是这个编译器本身，

speaker: Guest
text_en: is that it's built in a manner that where it can function in a highly interactive mode, which is what the IDE uses. Normally, you know, command line compilers, they just run through these stages and, you know, the output is just whatever gets emitted or some error messages, But in an IDE, you know, the compiler is a service. And what we do in that service is
text: 它的构建方式使它可以运行在一种高度交互的模式下，这也就是 IDE 所使用的模式。通常命令行编译器就是把这些阶段跑一遍，输出要么是生成的结果，要么是一些错误信息。但在 IDE 里，编译器其实是一项服务。而我们在这个服务里做的事情是

speaker: Guest
text_en: We basically take a program that is perpetually broken because you're typing. And yet we try to syntactically or semantically analyze it. And because we need to know when you press dot here, what could come next? Well, that means we need to know what is the type of the thing you dotted on. In order to figure that out, we may have to resolve stuff.
text: 我们基本上是在处理一个因为你不断输入而始终处于损坏状态的程序。但即便如此，我们还是要尝试对它做语法或语义分析。因为当你在这里按下点号时，我们得知道后面可能出现什么。那就意味着我们得知道你点的那个东西的类型是什么。为了搞清楚这一点，我们可能还得去做解析。

speaker: Guest
text_en: We may have to look at ASTs over here and whatever. And all of that has to happen within 200 milliseconds or else people think the IDE is slow, Well, what if you have 500,000 lines of code You can't compile all of those in 200 milliseconds. So you got to be super, super deferred and interactive. And so you got to do minimal amounts of work.
text: 我们可能得去看这边的 AST 之类的东西。而且这一切都必须在 200 毫秒内完成，不然大家就会觉得 IDE 很慢。可如果你有 50 万行代码呢？你不可能在 200 毫秒内把这些全编译完。所以你就必须做得非常非常延迟、非常交互式。也就是说，你必须把工作量压到最低。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And that's how our compiler is built is it tries to front load. Like, for example, like you have 500,000 lines of code. Well, let's say in 500 files. Well, we could build the ASTs for 499 of the files and just sit on them. We don't have to rebuild those because you're not editing in those files. We just have to update the AST of the current file you're in.
text: 我们的编译器就是这么构建的，它会尽量把工作前置。比如说，你有 50 万行代码，分布在 500 个文件里。那我们可以先把其中 499 个文件的 AST 建好并缓存起来。因为你又没有在编辑那些文件，所以没必要重建它们。我们只需要更新你当前所在文件的 AST。

speaker: Guest
text_en: So that goes 500 times faster than if we had to do all of it. And then we don't actually have to figure out all of the types in here either. We can just start where you're at and then just resolve just enough to answer the question that you're needing an answer for right now. And so everything is lazy and deferred and functional and reusable. inside the compiler.
text: 这样一来，速度就会比每次全做一遍快 500 倍。然后，我们其实也不需要把这里面所有类型都推导出来。我们可以只从你当前所在的位置开始，只解析出足够回答你眼下这个问题所需的那部分内容。所以编译器内部的一切都是惰性的、延迟的、函数式的，而且可复用。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And it's a very different way of writing compilers than what the textbooks will traditionally teach you.
text: 这和教科书里传统上教你的那种编译器写法非常不一样。

speaker: Host
text_en: Yeah, because I guess this is now, these are interactive compilers, if you will, right? It sounds like it's more than a compiler or a lot more difficult problem to solve.
text: 对，因为我猜这已经算是交互式编译器了，对吧？听起来这已经不只是编译器了，或者说这是个难得多的问题。

speaker: Guest
text_en: The same engine is there, but you got to build it in a manner where it can be very interactive. And that was not typically important for compilers, you know.
text: 底层还是同一个引擎，但你得把它构建成一种可以高度交互的方式。而这通常并不是编译器过去特别看重的东西。

speaker: Host
text_en: And so TypeScript is a superset of JavaScript. What are some features you would try to add if only JavaScript would allow it or if you were able to influence JavaScript's roadmap? What is something that you feel could make TypeScript a lot better? But of course, there's a constraint there.
text: 所以 TypeScript 是 JavaScript 的超集。如果只要 JavaScript 允许，或者你能影响 JavaScript 的路线图，你会想加哪些特性？你觉得什么东西能让 TypeScript 变得更好？当然，这里面也有约束。

speaker: Guest
text_en: We track the ECMAScript committee and and new language features that get developed in ECMAScript, we implement once they reach stage three or four in the standardization committee. And then we've sort of been on that train ever since the beginning. So there is a pipeline that supplies new language features in a standardized manner.
text: 我们会持续跟进 ECMAScript 委员会，ECMAScript 里开发出来的新语言特性，一旦在标准化委员会里进入第三阶段或第四阶段，我们就会去实现。从一开始我们基本上就一直在跟着这趟车走。所以确实有一条以标准化方式提供新语言特性的管道。

speaker: Guest
text_en: We sort of see it as our purview to define the type system on top, right? So that is, if you will, our playground. Now, I still have things that I wish I could have in the language itself. I mean, I like functional programming. I like functional programming languages. And key to them is that everything is an expression. There's really no distinction between statements and expressions.
text: 我们基本上认为，在这之上定义类型系统是我们的职责，对吧？所以如果你愿意这么说，那就是我们的游乐场。当然，我还是有一些希望语言本身能具备的东西。我的意思是，我喜欢函数式编程，也喜欢函数式编程语言。而它们的关键点之一就是一切都是表达式。语句和表达式之间其实没有真正的区别。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And so one of the features that JavaScript lacks in my estimate is the ability to give symbolic names to temporary results and expressions and then reuse them. This is the let let blah equals whatever in some expression that functional programming languages, you know, like camel and whatever all have. And it's nice because you could just stay in an expression context and you can just
text: 所以在我看来，JavaScript 缺少的一个特性，就是能给临时结果和表达式起符号化的名字，然后再复用它们。也就是那种 let let 某个东西等于什么什么，然后放在某个表达式里的能力。函数式编程语言里，比如 OCaml 之类的，基本都有这个。它很好的一点是，你可以始终待在表达式上下文里，然后你就可以

speaker: Guest
text_en: dot things together and whatever, and sort of do this more fluent style of programming. But then all of a sudden you need a name for something you want to reuse. And now you've got to pop out and declare a variable or turn it into state. Anyway. You know, that's one thing that I would like to fix. There's, there's something called do expressions that may or may not happen at some point,
text: 把各种东西串起来之类的，用一种更流畅的编程风格来写。但突然之间你需要给某个想复用的东西起个名字，这时你就得跳出来声明一个变量，或者把它变成状态。总之，这是我很想修掉的一点。有个东西叫 do expressions，未来某个时候也许会有，也许不会，

speaker: Guest
text_en: but it's taken a long time. So anyway, but I mean, generally speaking, I think JavaScript is a, it's a nice little language. It just has some issues, you know, and then, and I think we're very good at teasing them out with our type checker. And so, so once you have a checker that can warn you, Hey, you about to do something stupid here, then It's not so bad. The thing that makes it interesting,
text: 但这事已经拖了很久了。所以总的来说，我觉得 JavaScript 还是一门挺不错的小语言。它只是有一些问题，你知道的。而且我觉得我们很擅长通过类型检查器把这些问题揪出来。所以一旦你有了一个检查器，能提醒你：嘿，你这儿马上要干蠢事了，那其实也就没那么糟。真正让它有意思的地方，

speaker: Guest
text_en: I think, and unlike pretty much any other programming language, is the gradual typing. This notion that you can have types, but you don't have to have types. Other languages force you to type everything, right? Because they in turn use that information to generate machine code, you know, based on what the type is, you know, different instructions for float versus int versus whatever,
text: 我觉得，而且这点和几乎所有其他编程语言都不一样，就是渐进式类型。也就是你可以有类型，但你也可以不写类型。其他语言会强制你把所有东西都标上类型，对吧？因为它们反过来要利用这些信息来生成机器码，你知道的，要根据类型来决定，float、int 之类的会用不同的指令。

speaker: Guest
text_en: where in JavaScript, The types are there purely for the development experience and the checking. When the program runs, they're all gone. Now, of course, there are still types, but they're all dynamically computed. But that's kind of interesting because that means in the language, we don't necessarily have to prove 100% correctness.
text: 而在 JavaScript 里，类型纯粹是为了开发体验和类型检查而存在的。等程序真正运行时，它们就全都没了。当然，严格来说还是有“类型”的，但那些都是在运行时动态计算出来的。不过这点挺有意思的，因为这意味着在这门语言里，我们不一定非得证明 100% 的正确性。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And a lot of language features that we have, we can't 100% prove correctness. Like in a structural type system with recursive types, there are just cases that you can't analyze because the types are infinitely recurring. The more you try to relate to types, the deeper you go and you're just staring into the recursive abyss. You know what I mean?
text: 而且我们现在很多语言特性，本来也没法 100% 证明正确。比如在带递归类型的结构类型系统里，就会有一些情况你根本没法分析，因为类型会无限递归下去。你越想把它和类型关系理清，就会陷得越深，最后就像盯着递归的深渊看。你懂我意思吧？

speaker: Guest
text_en: But you can kind of go, well, we've proven it to four levels. That's probably good enough. We're just going to say it's good enough. And then, you know, if everything else works out, we're going to go, sure. That you can't do if you were to go generate machine code that then would have indeterminate behavior, But if JavaScript has a runtime where everything is well-defined already,
text: 但你可以说，好吧，我们已经证明到四层了，那大概就够用了。我们就认定这已经足够好了。然后你知道，如果其他地方也都没问题，我们就说，行，可以。这种做法如果是去生成机器码就不行了，因为那样可能会导致不确定的行为。但如果 JavaScript 本身就有一个一切都已明确定义好的运行时，

speaker: Guest
text_en: So if we're checking 99% instead of 100%, heck, that's better than the 0% that JavaScript checked, And it gives you language features that no other languages can provide because they can't get to 100%.
text: 所以就算我们检查到的是 99% 而不是 100%，那也比 JavaScript 原本的 0% 检查强多了。而且这还能给你带来其他语言做不到的语言特性，因为它们非得做到 100% 才行。

speaker: Host
text_en: It's interesting how constraints lead to innovation or even limitations can lead to more innovation. Speaking of innovation, one of the biggest innovations that is everywhere is the AI agents, AI coding tools that us software engineers, most software engineers are using, increasingly using AI agents as well. As you're developing languages on a more, I guess, niche team.
text: 很有意思，约束会催生创新，甚至限制本身也可能带来更多创新。说到创新，现在到处都是的一大创新就是 AI agents、AI 编码工具。我们软件工程师，或者说大多数软件工程师，都在用，而且也越来越多地开始用 AI agents。像你们这种做语言开发、相对更偏细分的团队，

speaker: Host
text_en: What kinds of AI tools are you using or how is AI helping your language development work? May that be TypeScript or C Sharp?
text: 你们会用哪些 AI 工具？或者说，AI 是怎么帮助你们做语言开发工作的？不管是 TypeScript 还是 C Sharp。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Day-to-day, I work on TypeScript and I can certainly talk about how we've been in the process of moving TypeScript to native code for the last year and a half or so. In the beginning of that project, AI was nowhere near as capable as it is now, and therefore we could not really use much of it in the beginning. At this point, though, I'd say we're using AI fairly well. Obviously, we're on GitHub.
text: 我日常主要是在做 TypeScript，我当然也可以讲讲，过去大概一年半里，我们一直在把 TypeScript 迁移到原生代码。这个项目刚开始的时候，AI 远没有现在这么强，所以一开始我们其实基本用不上它。不过到了现在，我会说我们已经把 AI 用得相当不错了。很明显，我们是在 GitHub 上工作的。

speaker: Guest
text_en: We use AI to code review, pull requests. That in the beginning was not all that great, but now it's actually getting a lot better. We use AI to implement issues or fix issues, simple issues. And then it succeeds some of the time in this port that we're doing, you know, because we snapped a copy of the source code from a year and a half ago and then ported it.
text: 我们会用 AI 做代码审查、看 pull request。这个在一开始并不算特别好，但现在确实已经进步很多了。我们也会用 AI 来实现 issue，或者修一些简单的问题。在我们现在做的这个移植项目里，它有时候也确实能成功，因为我们是基于一年半前的一份源码快照，然后在那个基础上做移植。

speaker: Guest
text_en: We have a backlog of PRs that need to be moved on to the new native compiler. And so we're using AI to help us move those pull requests. And that's actually going fairly well at this point. And then we use it for a bunch of grudgery, drudgery work. Like, okay, here's this feature. Please write me some tests in the same style as these other tests, right? And kaboom.
text: 我们有一批积压的 PR 需要迁移到新的原生编译器上。所以我们在用 AI 帮忙推进这些 pull request。到目前为止，实际效果还挺不错的。然后我们也会拿它来做一堆苦活累活。比如，好，这里有个功能，请你按照其他这些测试的风格给我写一些测试，对吧？然后砰，一下就出来了。

speaker: Guest
text_en: It's like no one likes writing tests. AI loves writing tests and it'll just pump out more tests and great, you know. So we're trying to use it to get rid of all the toil that otherwise we would spend our time on. But I would say we're not at a point where it absolves us from understanding what we're doing. Not at all.
text: 就像没人喜欢写测试。AI 可太喜欢写测试了，它会源源不断地吐出更多测试，这很好，你懂的。所以我们是在尽量用它把那些原本要占用我们时间的脏活累活都干掉。但我得说，我们还远没到可以因此就不用理解自己在做什么的地步。完全不是。

speaker: Host
text_en: plus your level at the stack, if you will, because you're building a language, it might argue that someone really needs to understand at least one person. Ideally, the whole team needs to understand those fundamental parts, right?
text: 再加上你们在技术栈里所处的层级，毕竟你们是在做一门语言，这可能更说明至少得有一个人真正理解这些东西。理想情况下，整个团队都得理解这些底层基础部分，对吧？

speaker: Guest
text_en: Language is interesting in the world of AI because AI... We wouldn't have this conversation if it wasn't for languages, because how would AI get to determinism without programming languages, I mean, AI is by design stochastic and indeterminate. It might give you a different answer the next time you ask it the same question,
text: 在 AI 的世界里，语言这件事很有意思，因为 AI……如果没有语言，我们根本不会有这场对话，因为没有编程语言的话，AI 怎么可能实现确定性呢？我的意思是，AI 从设计上就是随机的、非确定的。你下次问它同一个问题，它都可能给你一个不一样的答案，

speaker: Guest
text_en: either just because random or because there's a new model or there's a whatever. There's no determinism.
text: 可能只是因为随机，也可能是因为换了个新模型，或者别的什么原因。它没有确定性。

speaker: Guest
text_en: We can't build applications if they have non-deterministic behavior. I mean, what would a banking app look like if it decided to hallucinate or whatever, right? So you have to have something where the rubber reads the road and where you can reason about and where you can replicate the behavior. Every time you run the app, the same thing happens.
text: 如果应用的行为是非确定性的，我们根本没法构建应用。我的意思是，如果一个银行 App 动不动就开始幻觉乱说，那会是什么样，对吧？所以你必须得有某种真正落地的东西，让你能够推理它的行为，也能够复现它的行为。每次运行这个 App，发生的都得是同样的事。

speaker: Host
text_en: I mean, I even see it in, I think, almost all AI agents or tools these days when you ask it, something to do with data. Oftentimes they will start writing a Python program because I think the AI designers figured out that you at some point want to turn some non-deterministic into a deterministic and what is the thing that we know most efficient? Don't ask it for the answer.
text: 我的意思是，我甚至觉得现在几乎所有 AI agent 或工具里都能看到这一点：当你让它处理跟数据有关的事情时，它往往会开始写一个 Python 程序，因为我觉得 AI 的设计者已经意识到，你终归会想把某些非确定性的东西变成确定性的。而我们已知最高效的办法是什么？不是直接问它答案。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Ask it to write a program that computes the answer.
text: 而是让它写一个程序来算出答案。

speaker: Host
text_en: And you will know that that will be deterministic.
text: 而且你会知道，那会是确定性的。

speaker: Host
text_en: It's very interesting. But speaking of languages for AI, a question that comes up, of course, because AI is everywhere is generating a lot more code. What is your take on either modifying existing languages for AI usage based on what you're seeing, the patterns, or potentially coming up with, would it make any sense to come up with a language that is more suited for AI agents to use?
text: 这很有意思。不过说到面向 AI 的语言，当然会冒出一个问题，因为 AI 现在无处不在，也在生成越来越多的代码。结合你看到的这些现象和模式，你怎么看：是去修改现有语言，让它们更适合 AI 使用，还是说，干脆设计一种更适合 AI agents 使用的语言，这件事有意义吗？

speaker: Guest
text_en: Well, my flippant answer there is, you know, that The language that's most suited for AI is the language that AI has seen the most of in its training set, And that's why you could argue AI does really well on JavaScript and TypeScript and Python, because it's seen an awful lot of it. And there's an awful lot of that still. And then, and so, and that just reinforces itself, right?
text: 嗯，我先说个不那么严肃的回答吧。你知道，对 AI 来说最合适的语言，就是它在训练集里见得最多的那种语言。所以你可以说，AI 在 JavaScript、TypeScript 和 Python 上表现特别好，就是因为它见过海量这类代码。而且现在这类代码依然非常多。于是这件事就会自我强化，对吧？

speaker: Guest
text_en: And you could argue, the reason TypeScript and JavaScript are popular, that's mostly to do with the browser and not so much to do with AI, But it's interesting to look at why is AI targeting TypeScript versus just JavaScript? And there, I think the types actually help guide the AI to producing better programs. And I think our combination of the ability to type something when there's no context
text: 你也可以说，TypeScript 和 JavaScript 之所以流行，主要还是因为浏览器，而不是因为 AI。但有意思的是，为什么 AI 更偏向生成 TypeScript，而不只是 JavaScript？我觉得这里类型系统确实能帮助引导 AI 产出更好的程序。而且我认为，我们这种在没有上下文时可以显式写类型、而在有上下文时又能做类型推断的组合，刚好非常合适，因为如果你强迫 AI 给所有东西都写上类型注解，

speaker: Guest
text_en: but also our ability to infer it when there is context is just the right combination because if you were to force AI to write a type annotation on everything, then it would probably get it wrong more often because now it has to keep track of all these types and it has to just repeat itself over and over and over, And so types are important where there's no context,
text: 那它出错的概率反而可能更高，因为它现在得一路跟踪所有这些类型，还得一遍又一遍地重复自己。所以，在没有上下文的时候，类型很重要；

speaker: Guest
text_en: but inference is super important for the dry or do not repeat yourself principle, And fewer tokens generally makes AI more efficient. And so I think we have a very nice combo there in how you can just sort of type the outermost parameter and then everything flows. from there on in.
text: 但为了贯彻 DRY，也就是“不要重复自己”这个原则，类型推断又特别重要。而且 token 更少，通常也会让 AI 更高效。所以我觉得我们这里的组合非常好：你只需要给最外层的参数标一下类型，后面的东西就都能顺着推出来。

speaker: Host
text_en: Now, one thing that AI is already revolting in, again, for the GitHub Team Share stats, so this is also open data, that AI agents are generating a lot more code. I mean, they're both quick to generate. They also like to be sometimes verbose. Knowing that we are already seeing a lot more code pushed everywhere at a project level, at an aggregate level.
text: 现在有一件事是 AI 已经在彻底搅动的。还是引用 GitHub Team Share 的统计，这也是公开数据：AI agents 正在生成越来越多的代码。它们生成得很快，而且有时候也挺啰嗦。既然我们已经看到，在项目层面、在整体汇总层面，到处都在被提交更多代码。

speaker: Host
text_en: What do you think language characteristics could become more important in this world of just a lot more code, oftentimes generated by machines?
text: 你觉得在这样一个代码量大幅增加、而且很多还是机器生成的世界里，语言的哪些特性会变得更重要？

speaker: Guest
text_en: I mean, you could argue that we're already past peak truth on the internet, right? And now there's just more and more garbage every day. It gets harder and harder to suss out the stuff that you do want to include in the training set in order to actually make something more intelligent. So I think that gradual, I mean, I'm sure people are working on it. I could see that as becoming problematic.
text: 我的意思是，你完全可以说，互联网早就已经过了“真相峰值”，对吧？现在每天只会冒出越来越多垃圾内容。要从里面筛出那些你真的想放进训练集、从而真正做出更智能东西的内容，也变得越来越难。所以我觉得这种渐进式的问题——我是说，我相信已经有人在研究了——以后很可能会变得很棘手。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Languages that are suitable for AI, I think like I talked about types and inference. I think both of those are important. I think also locality is important. Locality? Well, what I mean by that is like don't have a bunch of global stuff. where AI has to grok the entire product. these pound include files that are, oh my God,
text: 我觉得适合 AI 的编程语言，像我刚才提到的类型和推断，这两点都很重要。我还觉得局部性也很重要。局部性？我的意思是，不要搞一堆全局的东西，让 AI 必须吃透整个产品。还有那些 #include 文件，天啊，

speaker: Guest
text_en: who knows where they're in scope and how do I put that in the context window or not? And then do I burn like a gazillion tokens on trying to include, but But if you have good locality where you're clearly stating what you're importing and whatever and you can analyze just a single source file and from that extract its protocol to the outside world without having to know anything deeper.
text: 谁知道它们到底在哪个作用域里？我到底要不要把这些东西塞进上下文窗口？那我是不是得烧掉海量 token 去硬塞这些内容？但如果你的局部性很好，导入了什么都写得很清楚之类的，那你就可以只分析单个源文件，并从中提取出它对外部世界的协议，而不需要知道更深层的东西。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Do you know what I mean? I think those are important aspects just simply to reduce the size of the of the context window and also make it easier to summarize each module in a program.
text: 你明白我的意思吧？我觉得这些都很重要，单纯就能减少上下文窗口的大小，也能让程序里的每个模块更容易被总结。

speaker: Host
text_en: This is so fascinating because I remember this was probably 15 years ago where PHP was very much critiqued for its globals. and early on I didn't understand as a young developer why that was a big deal I was just hacking around in PHP until I had the issue of something was not working and turns out that something imported over with a global and suddenly you realize that
text: 这太有意思了，因为我记得大概是 15 年前，PHP 因为它的全局变量问题被批评得很厉害。刚开始的时候，作为一个年轻开发者，我并不理解这为什么是个大问题。我当时就是随手用 PHP 瞎写，直到有一次碰到某个东西就是不工作，结果发现是某个通过全局变量导入进来的东西出了问题，然后你就突然意识到，

speaker: Host
text_en: when things are defined across the code base a global could be anywhere and there's no way for you to to know when someone else is doing your now back to the state problem which you just
text: 当定义散落在整个代码库里的时候，一个全局变量可能出现在任何地方，你根本没法知道别人什么时候动了你的东西。然后这就又回到了你刚才说的状态问题。

speaker: Guest
text_en: talked about. Original JavaScript suffered from this problem. There were no modules, right? Everything was global and anyone could just like monkey patch anything else. And it was impossible to know really, what am I sitting on top of here? But now with ECMAScript modules and whatever, we're moving towards sanity and more and more the world is written that way in the JavaScript ecosystem.
text: 没错。早期的 JavaScript 就有这个问题。那时候没有模块，对吧？所有东西都是全局的，谁都可以随手 monkey patch 别的东西。你根本不可能真正知道，我现在到底是建立在什么东西之上的？但现在有了 ECMAScript modules 之类的东西，我们正在朝着更正常、更理性的方向走，而且在 JavaScript 生态里，越来越多代码都是这么写的。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And that's a good thing. And I think that will help us down the line with AI.
text: 这是件好事。我觉得从长远来看，这也会对 AI 有帮助。

speaker: Guest
text_en: it's just starting to become aware of, you know, the existence of language services. And agents today like to use grep and awk and whatever, you know, to find all the places where you reference a certain thing, but it's not semantic search, And so if you have a common name for this property, like count or address or whatever, right? it's going to find a whole bunch of properties named address,
text: 它现在才刚开始意识到，你知道，语言服务的存在。现在的 agent 还是喜欢用 grep、awk 之类的工具，去找你在哪些地方引用了某个东西，但那不是语义搜索。所以如果这个属性的名字很常见，比如 count、address 之类的，对吧？它就会找到一大堆叫 address 的属性。

speaker: Guest
text_en: and then that's not going to work so well because now you don't know that you're renaming the right one, But this is where language services come in and semantic search. And I think that's going to increasingly become more important with AI. And really, these are services that are already provided by LSP implementations.
text: 这样效果就不太好了，因为你现在没法确定自己改名的是不是正确的那个。但这正是语言服务和语义搜索发挥作用的地方。我觉得随着 AI 的发展，这会变得越来越重要。而且说到底，这些服务其实已经由 LSP 的实现提供了。

speaker: Guest
text_en: but they may need some tweaking in order for them to be more accessible to AI. AI likes command line tools, you know, and they're not really command line tools.
text: 不过它们可能还需要做一些调整，才能让 AI 更容易用上。AI 喜欢命令行工具，但这些东西本质上又不太算命令行工具。

speaker: Host
text_en: And I also wonder if, for example, performance will be interesting because we know that these things can run faster, so faster feedback loops will be helpful, which we're now going back to one of the reasons that TypeScript was so popular, you said the 200 milliseconds of getting you feedback,
text: 我还在想，比如说性能会不会也很关键，因为我们知道这些东西其实可以跑得更快，所以更快的反馈回路会很有帮助。这样一来又回到了你刚才说的 TypeScript 当年那么受欢迎的一个原因，就是那种 200 毫秒就能给你反馈的体验。

speaker: Guest
text_en: But there are ways of, you know, where you could imagine, you know, like a server, keeping a project hot and giving your LSP services, you know, that AI can ask semantic questions and whatever. And then once AI stops asking after 10 minutes, the server just dumps it, you know, and whatever. There are ways of putting this together, I think, where we can make some progress on it. Because, like,
text: 不过还是有办法的。你可以想象，比如有个服务器一直把项目保持在热状态，然后提供 LSP 服务，让 AI 可以去问各种语义层面的问题之类的。等 AI 10 分钟都不再提问了，服务器就把它丢掉，之类的。我觉得这里面是有一些组合方式的，我们可以在这方面取得一些进展。因为像这样，

speaker: Guest
text_en: the ability for AI to semantically validate the code that it's generating as it's generating it will increasingly become important.
text: AI 在生成代码的同时，能够从语义层面对它生成的代码做校验，这种能力会变得越来越重要。

speaker: Host
text_en: What about the software craft? You've been in this industry for many decades, but it's hard to unsee that these tools are just coming to everyday use, similar to how at some point graphical IDs came before that, I guess higher level languages came. Knowing that AI agents and AI tools will be part of the craft, what do you think parts of software engineering craft will become less important
text: 那软件工艺本身呢？你在这个行业已经很多年了，但很难不注意到，这些工具现在正开始进入日常使用，就像当年先有了图形化 IDE，再往前我猜是有了更高级的语言。既然 AI agent 和 AI 工具都会成为这门手艺的一部分，你觉得软件工程这门工艺里，哪些部分会变得没那么重要，

speaker: Host
text_en: and what might become more important? In a sense, we're all turning into
text: 又有哪些会变得更重要？某种意义上，我们都在变成

speaker: Guest
text_en: project managers, right? And we can have an army of junior programmers called agents that will just spit out reams of code, but someone's got to have the big picture and review all of that. And so increasingly our craft is going from one of writing the code to one of reviewing the code and building the architecture of the code and overseeing the work. if you will. It's a different kind of craft.
text: 项目经理，对吧？我们可以有一大群叫作 agents 的初级程序员，源源不断地吐出成堆的代码，但总得有人把握全局、审核这一切。所以我们的这门手艺正越来越从“写代码”转向“审代码、搭代码架构、监督整体工作”。如果你愿意这么说的话。这是另一种手艺。

speaker: Guest
text_en: It's a different kind of enjoyment. I've always liked writing the code. To me, that was the fulfilling part, seeing it work. Do you know what I mean? And in a way, AI robs a little bit of that, right? I mean, because I am less interested in reviewing code,
text: 这也是另一种乐趣。我一直都喜欢亲手写代码。对我来说，那才是最有成就感的部分，看着它跑起来。你明白我的意思吧？而某种程度上，AI 确实会夺走一点这种感觉，对吧？我是说，因为我对审代码这件事没那么感兴趣，

speaker: Host
text_en: but I think we could also make the process of reviewing code much more interesting
text: 但我觉得我们也可以把审代码这个过程变得比现在有趣得多

speaker: Guest
